ABSTRACT The North Tianshan Belt in the southwestern Central Asian Orogenic Belt (CAOB) provides unique insights into crustal evolution and geodynamic processes of accretionary orogeny. Here, we present new structural, thermobarometric and geochronological data from the Xiaopu Metamorphic Complex (XMC) in the eastern North Tianshan. Structural analysis indicates that an initial shortening phase (D1), which produced NW‐SE‐striking folds, was followed by a sinistral transtension (D2). The latter is characterized by syn‐kinematic crystallization of peak mineral assemblages, suggesting an inverted Buchan‐type metamorphic sequence. Subsequent strike‐perpendicular extension (D3) likely facilitated the exhumation of the XMC. Phase modelling and geothermobarometry show a progressive increase in peak temperature from the andalusite–staurolite zone (~580°C) to the sillimanite–migmatite zone (~690°C) at nearly constant pressure (~4 kbar), indicating a high‐temperature and low‐pressure (HT‐LP) thermal regime consistent with an extensional setting. U–Pb dating on zircon, monazite, and apatite indicates that D2 occurred at ~330–310 Ma, coeval with the HT‐LP metamorphism (320–310 Ma), and that D3 occurred at ~285 Ma. Biotite 40 Ar/ 39 Ar dating confirms syn‐/post‐D3 cooling (298–275 Ma). We suggest that magmatic intrusion controlled by transtension at the mid‐crustal level and asymmetric exhumation likely formed the inverted Buchan‐type metamorphic sequence. Our new results, together with regional data, reveal the late Palaeozoic tectono‐metamorphic evolution of the southwestern CAOB, involving arc–arc accretion, back‐arc transtension, and post‐orogenic extension. These processes governed the tectonic transition from shortening to extension in the mid‐crust of arc systems and induced magmatic intrusion and crustal exhumation during the final stages of accretionary orogeny.

item.title: Metallogenesis and exploration vectors of the large-scale Tengjia gold deposit, Jiaodong Peninsula (Eastern China): Constraints from monazite u-pb geochronology and sulfide geochemistry
item.dc:creator: Jiayao Hao
Liuan Duan
Yu Zhang
Hongtao Zhao
Yongjun Shao
Yuncheng Guo
Xu Wang
Shuling Song
item.description: The Jiaodong Peninsula, China’s largest gold province (∼6,000 t) with significant deep exploration potential, has long been the subject of debate regarding ore sources and gold enrichment mechanisms. These uncertainties have hindered the development of a unified metallogenic model. This study integrates hydrothermal monazite U-Pb geochronology with pyrite and galena geochemistry to constrain metallogenesis and exploration vectors of the newly discovered altered rock-type Tengjia gold deposit (∼50 t @ 3.89 g/t) within the Zhaoping fault zone in the Jiaodong Peninsula. The deposit is characterized by three mineralization stages: pre-ore quartz-K-feldspar stage (Ⅰ), main-ore quartz-white micas-electrum-sulfides stage (Ⅱ), and post-ore quartz-calcite-galena-sphalerite-pyrite stage (Ⅲ). Hydrothermal monazite U-Pb dating of Stage Ⅱ mineralization yields an age of 125.5 ± 1.1 Ma (MSWD = 1.6), coincident with the regional emplacement of intermediate-basic dikes and the broader Early Cretaceous lithospheric thinning event in the eastern North China Craton. This temporal link is further supported by in-situ Pb isotope compositions of Stage Ⅱ galena, which indicate a possible ore source originating from an enriched lithospheric mantle, consistent with that of regional intermediate-basic dikes. These results suggest that the Tengjia gold deposit is genetically linked to Early Cretaceous mantle-derived magmatism associated with lithospheric thinning in the Jiaodong Peninsula. Compared with orebody-hosted Stage Ⅲ pyrite, Stage Ⅱ pyrite is depleted in Tl, As, Zn, Se, Co, Ni, and Te, indicating higher temperatures, higher oxygen fugacity (fO2), and lower pH conditions during the main mineralization stage. Elevated temperatures during Stage Ⅱ promoted intense fluid-rock interaction, and together with lower pH conditions, facilitated gold precipitation. Although increased fO2 theoretically inhibits gold precipitation, gold mineralization is temporally restricted to Stage Ⅱ, when intense fluid-rock interaction within beresitization zones promoted pyrite precipitation and the consumption of HS−, ultimately overriding the fO2 effect to trigger efficient gold deposition. Furthermore, Stage Ⅱ pyrite hosted within orebodies is systematically enriched in Ag (>0.15 ppm), As (>240 ppm), Sb (>0.09 ppm), and Ni, but depleted in Se (<1.2 ppm) relative to Stage Ⅱ wall-rock pyrite. These contrasts are controlled by physicochemical variations, with ore-proximal pyrite recording higher pH, lower fO2, lower sulfur fugacity, and relatively higher temperatures than those in altered wall-rock zones. This study provides novel genetic constraints on the Jiaodong gold deposits and highlights the potential of pyrite trace element geochemistry as an effective exploration vector for lode gold systems. Published: 2026-07-17T16:06:46+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.614 (consider)

item.title: Origin of the Caotan Fe-rich carbonatite in Qinling Orogenic Belt: Evidence from petrographic, mineralogical, and geochemical study
item.dc:creator: Danni Xu
Wenlei Song
Cheng Xu
Chunwan Wei
Shanna Xue
Le Qiao
Yajun Gao
item.description: Carbonatites are mantle-derived igneous rocks and important global resources of rare earth elements (REEs) and niobium (Nb). However, the processes responsible for the formation of iron (Fe)-rich carbonatites and their associated Fe mineralization remain poorly constrained. The Caotan carbonatites, located along the southern margin of the North China Block, are distinctly Fe-rich and lithologically diverse, comprising both calcite- and dolomite-dominated varieties. These features contrast sharply with nearby calcite-dominated carbonatites associated with Mo–REE–U–Pb polymetallic mineralization, such as those at Huanglongpu and Huayangchuan. This discrepancy suggests that the Caotan rocks followed a distinct magmatic evolutionary pathway, although their petrogenesis and the mechanisms responsible for Fe concentration remain unclear. In this study, detailed petrographic observations define an evolutionary sequence: dolomite–calcite carbonatite → dolomite carbonatite → magnetite–forsterite–calcite carbonatite → baryte- and tremolite-bearing calcite assemblages. Magnetite, the dominant Fe-bearing mineral, occurs as sparse, fine-grained crystals in the early dolomite-bearing carbonatites but becomes abundant in the magnetite–forsterite–calcite stage, where it forms euhedral to subhedral coarse phenocrysts containing ilmenite exsolution lamellae. Major-element and REE systematics of carbonates indicate intense fractional crystallization. Early crystallization and removal of Fe-poor calcite, accounting for up to approximately 75 vol% of the cumulate assemblage, progressively enriched the residual carbonatitic melt in Fe. Notably, in situ Sr isotopic analyses reveal a pronounced shift from less radiogenic initial values in the early carbonatites [(87Sr/86Sr)i = 0.70411–0.70457] to significantly more radiogenic ratios in later-stage magnetite–forsterite–calcite, baryte–calcite, and tremolite–calcite assemblages [(87Sr/86Sr)i = 0.70573–0.70604], indicating substantial crustal assimilation. We therefore propose that Fe enrichment was initially promoted by fractional crystallization of Fe-poor calcite, which progressively concentrated Fe in the residual melt. However, the critical trigger for magnetite saturation and Fe mineralization was subsequent interaction between the carbonatitic melt and surrounding wall rocks, leading to antiskarn formation, elevated silica activity, and ultimately magnetite precipitation. Published: 2026-07-15T15:35:55+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.601 (consider)

Carbonatites are mantle-derived igneous rocks and important global resources of rare earth elements (REEs) and niobium (Nb). However, the processes responsible for the formation of iron (Fe)-rich carbonatites and their associated Fe mineralization remain poorly constrained. These features contrast sharply with nearby calcite-dominated carbonatites associated with Mo–REE–U–Pb polymetallic mineralization, such as those at Huanglongpu and Huayangchuan. This discrepancy suggests that the Caotan rocks followed a distinct magmatic evolutionary pathway, although their petrogenesis and the mechanisms responsible for Fe concentration remain unclear. In this study, detailed petrographic observations define an evolutionary sequence: dolomite–calcite carbonatite → dolomite carbonatite → magnetite–forsterite–calcite carbonatite → baryte- and tremolite-bearing calcite assemblages. Magnetite, the dominant Fe-bearing mineral, occurs as sparse, fine-grained crystals in the early dolomite-bearing carbonatites but becomes abundant in the magnetite–forsterite–calcite stage, where it forms euhedral to subhedral coarse phenocrysts containing ilmenite exsolution lamellae. Major-element and REE systematics of carbonates indicate intense fractional crystallization. Early crystallization and removal of Fe-poor calcite, accounting for up to approximately 75 vol% of the cumulate assemblage, progressively enriched the residual carbonatitic melt in Fe. Notably, in situ Sr isotopic analyses reveal a pronounced shift from less radiogenic initial values in the early carbonatites [(87Sr/86Sr)i = 0.70411–0.70457] to significantly more radiogenic ratios in later-stage magnetite–forsterite–calcite, baryte–calcite, and tremolite–calcite assemblages [(87Sr/86Sr)i = 0.70573–0.70604], indicating substantial crustal assimilation. However, the critical trigger for magnetite saturation and Fe mineralization was subsequent interaction between the carbonatitic melt and surrounding wall rocks, leading to antiskarn formation, elevated silica activity, and ultimately magnetite precipitation.

item.title: Multi-component crustal recycling recorded by LiMg isotopes in Emeishan mafic–ultramafic layered intrusions with implications for Fe–Ti–V metallogeny
item.dc:creator: Yan-Fang Luo
Dong-Bo Tan
Hai-Ou Gu
Yang-Yang Wang
He Sun
Dong-Yong Li
Qian-Ling Zhou
Shi-Chu Zhao
Yilin Xiao
item.description: The Emeishan Large Igneous Province (ELIP), generated by Late Permian mantle plume activity, hosts world-class Fe–Ti–V deposits within mafic–ultramafic layered intrusions. Although increasing evidence suggests that crustal materials play an important role in ore formation, the nature and proportion of crustal components incorporated into the mantle sources of these intrusions remain poorly constrained. Here we present whole-rock major and trace element data together with Li, Mg, Sr, and Nd isotopic compositions for four representative ELIP intrusions (Panzhihua, Baima, Taihe, and Hongge) to address this issue. These intrusions exhibit trace-element patterns and SrNd isotopic characteristics comparable to those of ELIP high-Ti basalts, indicating derivation from a broadly common mantle source. The samples display large variations in Li isotopic compositions (δ7Li = −6.5‰ to 6.6‰), whereas Mg isotopic compositions (δ26Mg = −0.42‰ to −0.09‰) remain within the range of depleted mantle values. After excluding the effects of low-temperature processes, including surface weathering and shallow crustal contamination, we propose that the mantle sources of these intrusions were metasomatized by recycled crustal materials, including altered oceanic crust and sediments. Mass-balance modeling indicates contributions of approximately 0.15–8.25% altered oceanic crust, 1.06–5.48% terrigenous sediments, and 0.70–2.92% pelagic sediments to the mantle source. Recycling of these components likely introduced volatiles and metal elements into the plume source and increased its oxidation state, thereby creating favorable conditions for Fe–Ti–V mineralization in the ELIP. These results highlight the key role of crustal recycling in modifying mantle plume sources and enhancing the metallogenic potential of large igneous province magmatic systems. Published: 2026-07-15T15:35:57+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.598 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yan-Fang Luo; Dong-Bo Tan; Hai-Ou Gu; Yang-Yang Wang; He Sun; Dong-Yong Li; Qian-Ling Zhou; Shi-Chu Zhao; Yilin Xiao

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108713

Relevance: 0.598 (consider)

The Emeishan Large Igneous Province (ELIP), generated by Late Permian mantle plume activity, hosts world-class Fe–Ti–V deposits within mafic–ultramafic layered intrusions. Although increasing evidence suggests that crustal materials play an important role in ore formation, the nature and proportion of crustal components incorporated into the mantle sources of these intrusions remain poorly constrained. Here we present whole-rock major and trace element data together with Li, Mg, Sr, and Nd isotopic compositions for four representative ELIP intrusions (Panzhihua, Baima, Taihe, and Hongge) to address this issue. These intrusions exhibit trace-element patterns and SrNd isotopic characteristics comparable to those of ELIP high-Ti basalts, indicating derivation from a broadly common mantle source. The samples display large variations in Li isotopic compositions (δ7Li = −6.5‰ to 6.6‰), whereas Mg isotopic compositions (δ26Mg = −0.42‰ to −0.09‰) remain within the range of depleted mantle values. After excluding the effects of low-temperature processes, including surface weathering and shallow crustal contamination, we propose that the mantle sources of these intrusions were metasomatized by recycled crustal materials, including altered oceanic crust and sediments. Mass-balance modeling indicates contributions of approximately 0.15–8.25% altered oceanic crust, 1.06–5.48% terrigenous sediments, and 0.70–2.92% pelagic sediments to the mantle source. Recycling of these components likely introduced volatiles and metal elements into the plume source and increased its oxidation state, thereby creating favorable conditions for Fe–Ti–V mineralization in the ELIP. These results highlight the key role of crustal recycling in modifying mantle plume sources and enhancing the metallogenic potential of large igneous province magmatic systems.

item.title: Selenium content and enrichment processes recorded in the Magushan Cu-Mo skarn deposit, Xuancheng ore district, China
item.dc:creator: Yue Li
Feng Yuan
Simon M. Jowitt
Taofa Zhou
Xunyu Hu
Xiaohui Li
item.description: Selenium is a semi-metal that is often considered critical and is typically produced as a byproduct of copper production from porphyry-skarn deposits. The Magushan deposit is a skarn Cu-Mo deposit within the Middle and Lower Yangtze River metallogenic belt in China that contains significant amounts of Se mineralization. This study focuses on the distribution and deportment of Se within the deposit, the factors controlling the variations of Se in the sulfides present at Magushan, and the source of Se. The deposit records pre-ore, Cu-Mo, Pb-Zn, and post-ore stages of mineralization, with the majority of Se mineralization associated with the Cu-Mo stage. Except for a very small amount of naumannite (Ag2Se) formed during the Pb-Zn stage, the Se within the Magushan system was mainly incorporated into Cu-Mo stage sulfides by isomorphic substitution for S. The Se content of different sulfides in the system is also likely controlled by variations in distribution coefficients, with Se more likely to be incorporated into pyrite within the Magushan deposit than other sulfides. The sulfides formed during the Pb-Zn stage of mineralization have significantly lower Se concentrations (<50 ppm) than those formed during the Cu-Mo stage of mineralization (molybdenite: 311.9−715.5 ppm Se; chalcopyrite: 325.2−742.3 ppm; pyrite: 221.8−833.5 ppm). This indicates that the Se content of these sulfides is controlled by variations in temperature, oxygen fugacity, and the Se concentration of the hydrothermal fluids that formed the deposit. The Se/S ratios and δ34S values of sulfides from the Magushan deposit (1.1‰−5.4‰ with a mean of 2.5‰; N = 38) also provide evidence of the magmatic sourcing of S and Se. These data also suggest that the assimilation of upper crustal rocks by parental magmas associated with the Se-bearing deposit may be a crucial process for the generation of Se mineralization. In short, the Se mineralization within the Magushan skarn system is related to a proximal porphyritic granodiorite, with the Se within the deposit predominantly hosted by sulfides. The Se concentrations of these sulfides are controlled by variations in temperature, oxygen fugacity, distribution coefficient, and the Se concentration of the hydrothermal fluids that formed the deposit. These characteristics should be used to guide both the exploration for, and the assessment of, the Se potential of skarn systems elsewhere within the Middle and Lower Yangtze River metallogenic belt in China and globally. Published: 2026-07-16T18:50:51+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.595 (consider)

These characteristics should be used to guide both the exploration for, and the assessment of, the Se potential of skarn systems elsewhere within the Middle and Lower Yangtze River metallogenic belt in China and globally.

item.title: Late Paleozoic paleomagnetic constraints on the eastern segment of the Central Asian Orogenic Belt
item.dc:creator: Zhaoyang Zhou
Yongjiang Liu
Yuyang Chen
Qingbin Guan
Runkang Du
Tong Zhou
A.Yu. Peskov
A. Didenko
Shihong Zhang
Sanzhong Li
item.description: The eastern segment of the Central Asian Orogenic Belt comprises multiple continental blocks and accretionary terranes that were progressively assembled during subduction-accretion processes. However, reliable Devonian–Carboniferous paleomagnetic data capable of constraining its paleogeographic configuration remain scarce. Here we present new paleomagnetic data from Late Paleozoic sedimentary successions in NE China, including twelve sites from the Xing’an Accretionary Terrane and eight sites from the Jiamusi Block. Two stable characteristic remanent magnetizations (ChRMs) are isolated from the Early Devonian Huolongmen Formation (ca. 415–394 Ma) and the Early Carboniferous Beixing Formation (ca. 335 Ma), yielding paleolatitude ranges of 32.4–57.0°N and 33.5–49.8°N, respectively. The ChRM directions pass fold and/or reversal tests, indicating a primary origin. The rock magnetic analyses identify magnetite as the dominant carrier of the remanence. These results provide the first well-documented Devonian–Carboniferous paleomagnetic poles from Northeast China and constrain the paleogeographic configuration of the region during the Late Paleozoic, prior to the development of the NE China Orocline. Published: 2026-07-17T23:47:15+00:00 Venue: Geoscience Frontiers Score: 0.590 (consider)

Journal: Geoscience Frontiers

The eastern segment of the Central Asian Orogenic Belt comprises multiple continental blocks and accretionary terranes that were progressively assembled during subduction-accretion processes. However, reliable Devonian–Carboniferous paleomagnetic data capable of constraining its paleogeographic configuration remain scarce. Here we present new paleomagnetic data from Late Paleozoic sedimentary successions in NE China, including twelve sites from the Xing’an Accretionary Terrane and eight sites from the Jiamusi Block. Two stable characteristic remanent magnetizations (ChRMs) are isolated from the Early Devonian Huolongmen Formation (ca. 415–394 Ma) and the Early Carboniferous Beixing Formation (ca. 335 Ma), yielding paleolatitude ranges of 32.4–57.0°N and 33.5–49.8°N, respectively. The ChRM directions pass fold and/or reversal tests, indicating a primary origin. The rock magnetic analyses identify magnetite as the dominant carrier of the remanence. These results provide the first well-documented Devonian–Carboniferous paleomagnetic poles from Northeast China and constrain the paleogeographic configuration of the region during the Late Paleozoic, prior to the development of the NE China Orocline.

item.title: Iron redox and hydrogen generation during multistage serpentinization under low silica activity: insights from the Heimulin serpentinites, central China
item.dc:creator: Meijun Gong
Kai Wu
Xinru Zhu
Chang Zhang
Chao Zhang
Hong Zhang
Yang Hu
Juan Zhang
Honglin Yuan
Weidong Sun
item.description: The redistribution and oxidation of iron during serpentinization are critical for quantifying hydrogen production and redox fluxes in subduction zones. This study investigates serpentinites from the Heimulin area in the Qinling Orogen, central China, to constrain their evolution and identify the factors controlling Fe3+ incorporation into serpentine minerals and associated H2 generation. Whole-rock geochemical compositions and the chemistry of relict primitive Cr-spinel domains indicate that the protoliths were highly refractory forearc mantle peridotites. The enrichment patterns of fluid-mobile elements closely resemble those of forearc serpentinites such as those from the Mariana, suggesting the protolith was hydrated by slab-derived fluids in a shallow forearc region. Mineralogical analyses identify four distinct types of lizardite, reflecting the sequence of serpentinization. Initial low-temperature serpentinization produced some Type-1 lizardite grains, in which Fe3+ was incorporated into the crystal lattice through dioctahedral substitution. When silica activity became sufficiently low, hydration of olivine produced Fe-rich brucite and some Type-1 and Type-2 lizardite grains that lack evident Fe3+ substitution. As serpentinization progressed, local increases in silica activity, driven by mineralogical heterogeneity (e.g., orthopyroxene dissolution) or influx of SiO2-rich external fluids, induced breakdown of Fe-rich brucite and promoted the formation of fine-grained magnetite and some Type-2 lizardite grains, in which Fe3+ was incorporated into the crystal lattice through cronstedtite substitution. Since redox reactions primarily occur during interactions between brucite and SiO2-rich fluids, and given the widespread occurrence of cronstedtite-type substitution in natural lizardite, the formation of Fe3+-serpentine with tetrahedral Fe3+ may indicate that hydrogen production has reached the main stage. During subsequent recrystallization, the Fe3+-deficient environment favored the transformation of lizardite into chrysotile asbestos. Simultaneously, silica-poor conditions promoted the preservation of brucite, which aggregated and recrystallized to form economically significant fibrous brucite deposits. However, the chemical compositions of lizardite in recrystallized serpentinites, together with bulk-rock Fe3+/ΣFe ratios, indicate that Fe3+ was redistributed during recrystallization. Overall, low-temperature serpentinization of refractory peridotites under SiO2-poor conditions, followed by recrystallization, promotes the formation of economically valuable asbestos and brucite fibers. Published: 2026-07-14T23:19:41+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.587 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Meijun Gong; Kai Wu; Xinru Zhu; Chang Zhang; Chao Zhang; Hong Zhang; Yang Hu; Juan Zhang; Honglin Yuan; Weidong Sun

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.07.018

Relevance: 0.587 (consider)

The redistribution and oxidation of iron during serpentinization are critical for quantifying hydrogen production and redox fluxes in subduction zones. This study investigates serpentinites from the Heimulin area in the Qinling Orogen, central China, to constrain their evolution and identify the factors controlling Fe3+ incorporation into serpentine minerals and associated H2 generation. Whole-rock geochemical compositions and the chemistry of relict primitive Cr-spinel domains indicate that the protoliths were highly refractory forearc mantle peridotites. The enrichment patterns of fluid-mobile elements closely resemble those of forearc serpentinites such as those from the Mariana, suggesting the protolith was hydrated by slab-derived fluids in a shallow forearc region. Mineralogical analyses identify four distinct types of lizardite, reflecting the sequence of serpentinization. Initial low-temperature serpentinization produced some Type-1 lizardite grains, in which Fe3+ was incorporated into the crystal lattice through dioctahedral substitution. When silica activity became sufficiently low, hydration of olivine produced Fe-rich brucite and some Type-1 and Type-2 lizardite grains that lack evident Fe3+ substitution. As serpentinization progressed, local increases in silica activity, driven by mineralogical heterogeneity (e.g., orthopyroxene dissolution) or influx of SiO2-rich external fluids, induced breakdown of Fe-rich brucite and promoted the formation of fine-grained magnetite and some Type-2 lizardite grains, in which Fe3+ was incorporated into the crystal lattice through cronstedtite substitution. Since redox reactions primarily occur during interactions between brucite and SiO2-rich fluids, and given the widespread occurrence of cronstedtite-type substitution in natural lizardite, the formation of Fe3+-serpentine with tetrahedral Fe3+ may indicate that hydrogen production has reached the main stage. During subsequent recrystallization, the Fe3+-deficient environment favored the transformation of lizardite into chrysotile asbestos. Simultaneously, silica-poor conditions promoted the preservation of brucite, which aggregated and recrystallized to form economically significant fibrous brucite deposits. However, the chemical compositions of lizardite in recrystallized serpentinites, together with bulk-rock Fe3+/ΣFe ratios, indicate that Fe3+ was redistributed during recrystallization. Overall, low-temperature serpentinization of refractory peridotites under SiO2-poor conditions, followed by recrystallization, promotes the formation of economically valuable asbestos and brucite fibers.

item.title: Metamorphic P–T–t evolution and geodynamic implications of Neoarchean supracrustal rocks in the Western Shandong Province, North China Craton
item.dc:creator: Lingling Xiao
Guodong Wang
Zheng Shi
Ziyuan Chen
Yongpan Li
item.description: The Western Shandong Province (WSP) in the North China Craton (NCC) preserves extensive Neoarchean supracrustal rocks, which are key to understanding the early tectonic evolution of the eastern NCC. This study presents an integrated petrological, mineralogical and geochronological investigation on supracrustal rocks from the Taishan Group in the WSP. Phase equilibrium modeling and geothermobarometry results reveal that these rocks record consistent clockwise P–T paths. They experienced peak amphibolite-facies metamorphism under low- to medium-pressure conditions (∼4.5–5.7 kbar/600–700°C), followed by a retrograde stage characterized by near-isothermal decompression. LA-ICP-MS U-Pb dating of monazite constrains the timing of this metamorphic event to ∼ 2.53–2.50 Ga. Detrital zircon ages of ∼ 2.75 Ga and ∼ 2.55 Ga in a metapelite sample indicate provenance from both early and late Neoarchean crust and constrain the maximum depositional age of its sedimentary protolith to ∼ 2.55 Ga. The absence of medium- to high-pressure facies series metamorphism and the distinct clockwise P–T paths (compared to the anticlockwise paths reported for the high-grade Yishui Group) suggest that the ∼ 2.5 Ga tectonic regime in the WSP might not have been a simple continuation of an earlier mantle plume or a typical subduction-collision setting. Combined with regional data, this study favors a geodynamic model involving the amalgamation of micro-continents during the late Neoarchean in the WSP. Published: 2026-07-17T10:41:08+00:00 Venue: Precambrian Research Score: 0.586 (consider)

Relevance: 0.586 (consider)

The Western Shandong Province (WSP) in the North China Craton (NCC) preserves extensive Neoarchean supracrustal rocks, which are key to understanding the early tectonic evolution of the eastern NCC. This study presents an integrated petrological, mineralogical and geochronological investigation on supracrustal rocks from the Taishan Group in the WSP. Phase equilibrium modeling and geothermobarometry results reveal that these rocks record consistent clockwise P–T paths. They experienced peak amphibolite-facies metamorphism under low- to medium-pressure conditions (∼4.5–5.7 kbar/600–700°C), followed by a retrograde stage characterized by near-isothermal decompression. LA-ICP-MS U-Pb dating of monazite constrains the timing of this metamorphic event to ∼ 2.53–2.50 Ga. Detrital zircon ages of ∼ 2.75 Ga and ∼ 2.55 Ga in a metapelite sample indicate provenance from both early and late Neoarchean crust and constrain the maximum depositional age of its sedimentary protolith to ∼ 2.55 Ga. The absence of medium- to high-pressure facies series metamorphism and the distinct clockwise P–T paths (compared to the anticlockwise paths reported for the high-grade Yishui Group) suggest that the ∼ 2.5 Ga tectonic regime in the WSP might not have been a simple continuation of an earlier mantle plume or a typical subduction-collision setting. Combined with regional data, this study favors a geodynamic model involving the amalgamation of micro-continents during the late Neoarchean in the WSP.

item.title: Serpentinization parageneses controlled by lithological variations among olivine gabbro and peridotite from the Atlantis Massif, Mid-Atlantic Ridge
item.dc:creator: Toshio Nozaka
Shun Kazumata
Frieder Klein
item.description: Serpentinization of olivine impacts the geochemical and ecological environment via molecular hydrogen production, promoted by iron oxidation to form magnetite and ferric iron in serpentine. Previous studies suggested that serpentinization proceeds with various reactions and the extent of iron oxidation is variable depending on the reaction pathways. To examine the effect of lithological variation on reactions at an incipient stage of serpentinization, we carried out microscopic observations, electron probe analyses, and Raman spectroscopy of serpentine veins cutting olivine in gabbroic rocks and peridotites from the Atlantis Massif, Mid-Atlantic Ridge. The results indicate that the serpentine veins in the proximity of olivine dominantly comprise lizardite mixed with variable minor phases or components depending on igneous lithology: brucite in peridotites, troctolites, and primitive olivine gabbros; cronstedtite-bearing serpentine, commonly without brucite, in some troctolites and slightly evolved olivine gabbros; and neither brucite nor cronstedtite component in evolved olivine gabbros. This suggests that incipient-stage serpentinization paragenesis is dominantly controlled by silica activity reflecting lithological variation. In addition to magnetite, the formation of cronstedtite in gabbroic rocks has the potential to produce hydrogen. The presence or absence of brucite and cronstedtite could affect the timing and magnitude of iron oxidation and hydrogen production during serpentinization. Considering the predominance of gabbroic rocks in the lower oceanic crust, the variation in incipient-stage serpentinization paragenesis in gabbroic rocks may have an impact on the redox state and ecological environment around the (sub)seafloor. Published: 2026-07-17T23:43:38+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.581 (consider)

item.title: Controls on REE enrichment in post-collisional alkaline rocks by subduction-related metasomatism: insights from the Zhuopan Complex, southeastern Tibetan plateau
item.dc:creator: Zheng Liu
Lin Sun
Wei Zhang
Qing-He Yan
Guo-Chang Wang
Hao Liu
Han Zhang
Yan Yang
item.description: Alkaline complexes host important economic rare earth element (REE) resources which are of critical strategic importance in modern technology and industry. The REE mineralization associated with alkaline rocks is generally formed in the post-collision settings, but the mechanisms of REE enrichment in their source region remain poorly constrained. The Paleogene Zhuopan alkaline complex hosting the Yongping REE deposit was emplaced in a post-collisional setting following the Indo-Asian continental collision. This complex exhibits geochemical characteristics enriched in K2O and incompatible elements (including large-ion lithophile elements, high-field-strength elements, and REEs), coupled with enriched Sr-Nd-Hf isotopic compositions and crust-like REE and trace element signatures. The Zhuopan alkaline complex originated from low-degree partial melts of an enriched lithospheric mantle source (garnet-phlogopite-amphibole lherzolite) modified by subduction slab-released fluids, which subsequently underwent fractional crystallization. The geochemically consistent REE enrichment in the Zhuopan complex was not primarily controlled by shallow-level magmatic processes, but rather via preferential extraction from amphibole-bearing metasomatic mantle during melting events. Globally distributed alkaline rocks hosting REE deposits demonstrate geochemical affinities comparable to those of the Zhuopan complex. These findings highlight that ancient subduction-related metasomatic processes exert critical control on the formation of post-collisional alkaline rocks hosting REE deposits worldwide. Published: 2026-07-14T15:55:20+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.580 (consider)

Alkaline complexes host important economic rare earth element (REE) resources which are of critical strategic importance in modern technology and industry. The REE mineralization associated with alkaline rocks is generally formed in the post-collision settings, but the mechanisms of REE enrichment in their source region remain poorly constrained. The Paleogene Zhuopan alkaline complex hosting the Yongping REE deposit was emplaced in a post-collisional setting following the Indo-Asian continental collision. This complex exhibits geochemical characteristics enriched in K2O and incompatible elements (including large-ion lithophile elements, high-field-strength elements, and REEs), coupled with enriched Sr-Nd-Hf isotopic compositions and crust-like REE and trace element signatures. The Zhuopan alkaline complex originated from low-degree partial melts of an enriched lithospheric mantle source (garnet-phlogopite-amphibole lherzolite) modified by subduction slab-released fluids, which subsequently underwent fractional crystallization. The geochemically consistent REE enrichment in the Zhuopan complex was not primarily controlled by shallow-level magmatic processes, but rather via preferential extraction from amphibole-bearing metasomatic mantle during melting events. Globally distributed alkaline rocks hosting REE deposits demonstrate geochemical affinities comparable to those of the Zhuopan complex. These findings highlight that ancient subduction-related metasomatic processes exert critical control on the formation of post-collisional alkaline rocks hosting REE deposits worldwide.

item.title: The Complexity of Deep Aqueous Fluids in Planetary Ultramafic Mantles
item.dc:creator: Sophia E. Economon
Dimitri A. Sverjensky
item.description: Abstract Planetary volatiles cycle through a planet's crust, mantle, and atmosphere, playing a crucial role in surface processes and habitability. Discussions of planetary volatiles traditionally focus on water, carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur, but fluids in contact with silicate rocks and ultramafic mantles can contain significant amounts of magnesium and silicon. Ultramafic mantles may be common in our solar system and can be expected in many exoplanets. These environments have been studied through fluid inclusions in diamonds from Earth's upper mantle and in experiments of chloride‐free fluids in equilibrium with peridotite. It appears that magnesium‐chloride complexing is increasingly relevant at high temperatures and pressures, but such complexes are largely unconstrained for deep conditions. Effective models are needed to understanding volatile cycling on terrestrial worlds in our solar system and beyond. To address this, we analyzed published experimental data for silicate mineral solubilities in chloride‐rich fluids to assess fluid speciation in ultramafic planetary mantles. We regressed experimental solubilities of talc + quartz and tremolite + talc + quartz at 2 kb and 500–700°C, as well as solubilities of forsterite at 10 kb and 800–900°C to obtain new equilibrium constants for and revised equilibrium constants for several magnesium‐, calcium‐, and silicon‐bearing species. We then applied the DEW model to characterize the equilibrium constants over wide ranges of temperature and pressure. As a result, we produce a new and robust thermodynamic data set for magnesium‐, calcium‐, and silicon‐chloride complexes at upper mantle conditions which enable modeling of magnesium transport in deep planetary fluids. Published: 2026-07-18T19:00:46+00:00 Venue: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems Score: 0.580 (consider)

Journal: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems

Abstract Planetary volatiles cycle through a planet's crust, mantle, and atmosphere, playing a crucial role in surface processes and habitability. Discussions of planetary volatiles traditionally focus on water, carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur, but fluids in contact with silicate rocks and ultramafic mantles can contain significant amounts of magnesium and silicon. Ultramafic mantles may be common in our solar system and can be expected in many exoplanets. These environments have been studied through fluid inclusions in diamonds from Earth's upper mantle and in experiments of chloride‐free fluids in equilibrium with peridotite. It appears that magnesium‐chloride complexing is increasingly relevant at high temperatures and pressures, but such complexes are largely unconstrained for deep conditions. Effective models are needed to understanding volatile cycling on terrestrial worlds in our solar system and beyond. To address this, we analyzed published experimental data for silicate mineral solubilities in chloride‐rich fluids to assess fluid speciation in ultramafic planetary mantles. We regressed experimental solubilities of talc + quartz and tremolite + talc + quartz at 2 kb and 500–700°C, as well as solubilities of forsterite at 10 kb and 800–900°C to obtain new equilibrium constants for and revised equilibrium constants for several magnesium‐, calcium‐, and silicon‐bearing species. We then applied the DEW model to characterize the equilibrium constants over wide ranges of temperature and pressure. As a result, we produce a new and robust thermodynamic data set for magnesium‐, calcium‐, and silicon‐chloride complexes at upper mantle conditions which enable modeling of magnesium transport in deep planetary fluids.

item.title: First-principle study of mineral-solution equilibrium isotope fractionations of magnesium, sulfate and calcium in CaCO3
item.dc:creator: Chirantan Pramanik
Itay Halevy
item.description: Chemical impurities in CaCO 3 minerals, present as ion substitutions (e.g., Mg 2 + -for-Ca 2 + , SO 4 2 − -for-CO 3 2 − ) are common. Impurities incorporated into a carbonate lattice have a different physicochemical environment than their aqueous forms, resulting in isotopic fractionations of these impurity atoms between the mineral and its parent fluid. Moreover, the lattice distortion in the presence of impurities is proportional to the impurity concentrations, potentially leading to a concentration-dependent effect on the mineral-fluid isotopic fractionation of the impurities and the major elements in the minerals. A mechanistic understanding of the effects of impurities in CaCO 3 minerals on the mineral lattice and the isotopic fractionation of the impurities and major elements is necessary for the use of carbonate minerals and their trace elements as paleo-environmental archives. The difficulty in achieving isotopic equilibrium during experimental precipitation of carbonate minerals motivates the calculation of equilibrium isotope effects by ab initio density functional theory (DFT) methods. However, the utility of DFT calculations in constraining the effects of impurity elements in carbonate minerals on the isotopic fractionation of both the impurities themselves and the major elements in the mineral is limited by computational feasibility. Even a single substitution in a model lattice, composed of as many atoms as computationally feasible for DFT calculations, results in impurity concentrations that are higher than those typical of most natural and experimental samples. Using DFT, we systematically modeled a range of calcite and aragonite supercell sizes with a single Mg or carbonate-associated sulfate (CAS) impurity, allowing calculation of the dependence of the CaCO 3 -fluid isotopic fractionation on the impurity concentration. We studied the change in the impurity bonding environment and computed phonon frequencies to calculate the reduced partition function ratios and the fractionation of S, Mg and Ca isotopes between the CaCO 3 and its parent fluid. We obtained a near-constant CAS-aqueous SO 4 2 − S isotope fractionation in calcite of 2.9‰ over a range of CAS concentrations between 26400 and 58600 ppm (sulfate mole fractions of 1/36 and 1/16), in close agreement with the highest-CAS experimental value of 2.9‰. We found a constant mineral-aqueous Mg 2 + Mg isotope fractionation over the range of modeled impurity concentrations of ∼ –8.6‰ in calcite and ∼ –7.2 to –8.8‰ in aragonite, in agreement with previous computational studies with similar Mg concentrations. We find a difference of up to ∼ 2.1‰ in 1000ln 44 α calcite and 1000ln 44 α aragonite between pure calcite and aragonite supercells and supercells of those minerals with 1/24 and 1/32 mole fractions of sulfate, respectively. A difference of up to ∼ 1‰ of 1000ln 44 α calcite and 1000ln 44 α aragonite from pure calcite and aragonite is observed with similar mole fractions of magnesium. These results highlight the effects of varying impurity concentrations in CaCO 3 minerals on the mineral-solution equilibrium isotopic fractionations of impurities and major elements, and the importance of understanding such effects for interpreting major and trace element isotopic variability in environmental and geological samples. Published: 2026-07-14T06:18:44+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.579 (consider)

Chemical impurities in CaCO 3 minerals, present as ion substitutions (e.g., Mg 2 + -for-Ca 2 + , SO 4 2 − -for-CO 3 2 − ) are common. Impurities incorporated into a carbonate lattice have a different physicochemical environment than their aqueous forms, resulting in isotopic fractionations of these impurity atoms between the mineral and its parent fluid. Even a single substitution in a model lattice, composed of as many atoms as computationally feasible for DFT calculations, results in impurity concentrations that are higher than those typical of most natural and experimental samples. Using DFT, we systematically modeled a range of calcite and aragonite supercell sizes with a single Mg or carbonate-associated sulfate (CAS) impurity, allowing calculation of the dependence of the CaCO 3 -fluid isotopic fractionation on the impurity concentration. We studied the change in the impurity bonding environment and computed phonon frequencies to calculate the reduced partition function ratios and the fractionation of S, Mg and Ca isotopes between the CaCO 3 and its parent fluid. We obtained a near-constant CAS-aqueous SO 4 2 − S isotope fractionation in calcite of 2.9‰ over a range of CAS concentrations between 26400 and 58600 ppm (sulfate mole fractions of 1/36 and 1/16), in close agreement with the highest-CAS experimental value of 2.9‰. A difference of up to ∼ 1‰ of 1000ln 44 α calcite and 1000ln 44 α aragonite from pure calcite and aragonite is observed with similar mole fractions of magnesium. These results highlight the effects of varying impurity concentrations in CaCO 3 minerals on the mineral-solution equilibrium isotopic fractionations of impurities and major elements, and the importance of understanding such effects for interpreting major and trace element isotopic variability in environmental and geological samples.

item.title: Magmatic Fertility Indicators of the Chagai Arc: A Systematic Review of Published Whole-Rock Geochemistry and Implications for Porphyry Cu–Au Prospectivity
item.dc:creator: Khan, Mubashir
item.description: The porphyry copper–gold (Cu–Au) deposits of the world are the primary source for copper and are significant producers of gold and molybdenum, and the genesis is always linked to the geochemical characteristics of the arc magmas from which the deposits crystallised. The Chagai Arc of western Pakistan is a Neotethyan subduction-related magmatic belt with several significant porphyry prospects, namely the Reko Diq, the Saindak and the Koh-i-Sultan, which constitute one of the least-developed large porphyry Cu–Au provinces of the Tethyan metallogenic domain. The importance of the arc has not been assessed, however, as an integrated geochemical system within a fertility sensitive discrimination approach. Rock data sets already exist for each of the areas of the deposits, and these have been used and interpreted independently without a consistent criterion which makes it difficult to compare data from one area with another. Loucks, Henríquez and Fiorentini (2024, Economic Geology) proposed the whole-rock magmatic fertility framework, which is based on the ratios of Sr/Y, (La/Yb)N, Eu/Eu, V/Sc and Dy/Yb as proxies for fractionation depth and magmatic oxidation state, has not been applied to any arc segment in Pakistan. This study uses a set of validated, individually sourced published values (specifically Sr/Y and europium anomaly systematics by Richards et al. (2012) for ore-related Chagai and Iranian Tethyan intrusions, U–Pb zircon and molybdenite Re–Os geochronology by Wang et al. (2022) and Muhammad et al. (2024) for Saindak and Reko Diq, and compositional and petrogenetic data by Nicholson et al. (2010) for Koh-i-Sultan), not an independently re-compiled, sample-level multi-source database. The two rocks, Reko Diq and Saindak, have mean chondrite-normalised europium anomalies of Eu(N)/Eu = 0.88 and Sr/Y > 30, which are consistent with deep and hydrous differentiation that is preferred in the formation of porphyry Cu–Au-type ores under plagioclase-suppressed conditions (Richards et al., 2012). The evidence for the ore-forming event in the area of Saindak (~22.2 Ma) is much older than the main Reko Diq mineralising event (~11–12 Ma), suggesting that there was at least two distinct pulses of fertile magmas in this portion of the arc. Koh-i-Sultan (<2.5 Ma) is compositionally distinct (basaltic andesite to dacite, ordinary calc-alkaline trend) and has no published Sr/Y, Eu/Eu or V/Sc dataset available from this centre that could be found in this synthesis. This synthesis is the first application of the fertility discriminant scheme by Loucks et al. (2024) to a Pakistani arc system and it is concluded that the most direct path towards a fully quantitative, arc-scale map of the Chagai Belt fertility that could be used to guide porphyry exploration in the Chagai system and other similar Tethyan systems is through the re-extraction of samples from the cited datasets. Published: 2026-07-18T06:36:00+00:00 Venue: EarthArXiv Score: 0.577 (consider)

(2012) for ore-related Chagai and Iranian Tethyan intrusions, U–Pb zircon and molybdenite Re–Os geochronology by Wang et al. (2022) and Muhammad et al. (2024) for Saindak and Reko Diq, and compositional and petrogenetic data by Nicholson et al. (2010) for Koh-i-Sultan), not an independently re-compiled, sample-level multi-source database. The two rocks, Reko Diq and Saindak, have mean chondrite-normalised europium anomalies of Eu(N)/Eu = 0.88 and Sr/Y > 30, which are consistent with deep and hydrous differentiation that is preferred in the formation of porphyry Cu–Au-type ores under plagioclase-suppressed conditions (Richards et al., 2012). (2024) to a Pakistani arc system and it is concluded that the most direct path towards a fully quantitative, arc-scale map of the Chagai Belt fertility that could be used to guide porphyry exploration in the Chagai system and other similar Tethyan systems is through the re-extraction of samples from the cited datasets.

item.title: Geochemical constraints on the Yangtze–Cathaysia block boundary from early paleozoic I-type granitoids across the Chenzhou–Linwu fault
item.dc:creator: Yi-Xin Liu
Xiu-Quan Miao
Yang Yu
Xiao-Long Huang
item.description: Pre-existing sutures, as the fundamental lithospheric boundaries, potentially represent lithospheric weakened zones that are easily to be reactivated and transformed, playing a crucial role in the evolution of continental blocks. In the South China Block (SCB), the Neoproterozoic suture between the Yangtze and Cathaysia blocks is well defined in the northeast along the Jiangshan–Shaoxing–Pingxiang fault (JSPF). However, whether this suture extends southward to the Chenzhou–Linwu fault (CLF) remains controversial, limiting our understanding of the tectonic evolution of the SCB. Thus, we present zircon UPb ages and HfO isotopic data, together with whole-rock geochemistry and SrNd isotopic data, for the Shangbao and Guidong granitoids located east of the CLF and the Daning granitoids situated to the west, respectively. Zircon UPb ages indicate that the studied granitoids were emplaced at ca. 443–435 Ma. All granitoids are I-type granitoids derived from the partial melting of ancient igneous basement. The high Y + Nb contents (144–593 ppm) of these granitoids, a planar distribution of Early Paleozoic granites in the SCB, and the absence of subduction-related records collectively support an intracontinental orogen for the Wuyi-Yunkai orogen (WYO). Furthermore, the Shangbao and Guidong granitoids, together with western Cathaysia I-type granitoids, exhibit lower Pb, Th, and U concentrations, and more negative εNd(t) and εHf(t) values than the Daning granitoids and eastern Yangtze I-type granitoids. This highlights a compositional contrast in crustal basements between the eastern and western segments of the CLF, characterized by minimal juvenile input in the former and a juvenile materials-modified basement in the latter. These findings suggest that the boundary between the Yangtze and Cathaysia blocks extends southward to the CLF, providing insights into the orogenic processes of the WYO, and the role of weak lithospheric boundaries in controlling asthenospheric upwelling and emplacement of mantle-derived magmas. Published: 2026-07-16T05:29:54+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.575 (consider)

This highlights a compositional contrast in crustal basements between the eastern and western segments of the CLF, characterized by minimal juvenile input in the former and a juvenile materials-modified basement in the latter. These findings suggest that the boundary between the Yangtze and Cathaysia blocks extends southward to the CLF, providing insights into the orogenic processes of the WYO, and the role of weak lithospheric boundaries in controlling asthenospheric upwelling and emplacement of mantle-derived magmas.

item.title: Fe–Mg isotope constraints on mantle processes and tectonic evolution of the Yilashan ophiolite, Tibet, China
item.dc:creator: Xiangzhen Xu
Fahui Xiong
Basem Zoheir
Tian Qiu
Huidan Xie
Weibin Gui
Jingsui Yang
item.description: The mantle processes and tectonic evolution of ophiolites in the Bangong–Nujiang suture zone remain debated, particularly regarding melt extraction, metasomatism, and chromitite formation in supra-subduction zone (SSZ) settings. Conventional tracers often fail to distinguish overprinting processes. Fe–Mg stable isotopes, which are sensitive to both partial melting and fluid/melt–rock interaction, offer a novel tool to fingerprint these processes. Here we present integrated Fe–Mg isotopic data, whole-rock geochemistry, and platinum-group element (PGE) systematics for harzburgites and chromitites from the Yilashan ophiolite (central Bangong–Nujiang suture zone) to address these issues. Harzburgites display near-chondritic δ56Fe values (0.02–0.09‰) and limited δ26Mg variability (–0.06 to 0.10‰), consistent with residues after high-degree melt extraction. Their depleted major element compositions, together with enrichment in large-ion lithophile elements and depletion in high-field-strength elements, require subsequent subduction-related metasomatism. In contrast, chromitites exhibit systematically lighter δ56Fe values (down to –0.31‰) and a wide range of δ26Mg values (–0.25 to 0.29‰), indicating pronounced isotopic heterogeneity. This variability is best explained by melt–rock interaction within focused melt pathways, consistent with the spatial association of chromitites with dunite channels, whereas alteration effects are not dominant but cannot be fully excluded. PGE systematics, including IPGE enrichment and distinct Pd/Ir–Pt/Pt* relationships, support chromitite formation from primitive boninitic melts, while harzburgite PGE patterns indicate melt infiltration or refertilization. Together, these data define a multi-stage evolutionary framework involving mantle depletion, subduction-related metasomatism, chromitite formation, and subsequent modification during cooling. Given current data limitations, this framework should be regarded as a working hypothesis requiring further testing. Published: 2026-07-15T15:41:57+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.574 (consider)

Journal: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences

DOI: 10.1016/j.jseaes.2026.107220

Relevance: 0.574 (consider)

The mantle processes and tectonic evolution of ophiolites in the Bangong–Nujiang suture zone remain debated, particularly regarding melt extraction, metasomatism, and chromitite formation in supra-subduction zone (SSZ) settings. Conventional tracers often fail to distinguish overprinting processes. Fe–Mg stable isotopes, which are sensitive to both partial melting and fluid/melt–rock interaction, offer a novel tool to fingerprint these processes. Here we present integrated Fe–Mg isotopic data, whole-rock geochemistry, and platinum-group element (PGE) systematics for harzburgites and chromitites from the Yilashan ophiolite (central Bangong–Nujiang suture zone) to address these issues. Harzburgites display near-chondritic δ56Fe values (0.02–0.09‰) and limited δ26Mg variability (–0.06 to 0.10‰), consistent with residues after high-degree melt extraction. Their depleted major element compositions, together with enrichment in large-ion lithophile elements and depletion in high-field-strength elements, require subsequent subduction-related metasomatism. In contrast, chromitites exhibit systematically lighter δ56Fe values (down to –0.31‰) and a wide range of δ26Mg values (–0.25 to 0.29‰), indicating pronounced isotopic heterogeneity. This variability is best explained by melt–rock interaction within focused melt pathways, consistent with the spatial association of chromitites with dunite channels, whereas alteration effects are not dominant but cannot be fully excluded. PGE systematics, including IPGE enrichment and distinct Pd/Ir–Pt/Pt* relationships, support chromitite formation from primitive boninitic melts, while harzburgite PGE patterns indicate melt infiltration or refertilization. Together, these data define a multi-stage evolutionary framework involving mantle depletion, subduction-related metasomatism, chromitite formation, and subsequent modification during cooling. Given current data limitations, this framework should be regarded as a working hypothesis requiring further testing.

item.title: Organic matter enrichment dynamics in North China Block: Implications for carbon cycle perturbations during the late Paleozoic Ice Age deglaciation
item.dc:creator: Yanan Li
Jiao Fang
Shuai Wang
Zhaorui Ye
Xiongxiong Li
item.description: The mechanisms governing carbon cycle feedbacks during the demise of the Late Paleozoic Ice Age (LPIA) remain poorly constrained, particularly the role of regional carbon sinks transitioning to sources. The North China Block (NCB), once a major late Paleozoic carbon sink, experienced a dramatic decline in carbon sequestration during the Sakmarian–Artinskian, coincident with global deglaciation. However, the drivers of this regional shift and its potential contribution to the LPIA termination are poorly constrained. To address this, an integrated petrological, sedimentological, and geochemical study was conducted on mudstones from the Dacheng coalfield, Hebei Province, establishing an organic matter (OM) enrichment model for the coastal plain to alluvial plain transition. Results indicate that Sakmarian coastal plain mudstones, deposited under warm-humid conditions with enhanced nutrient supply and suboxic-anoxic waters, host higher OM. In contrast, Artinskian alluvial plain deposits, influenced by climatic aridification, reduced nutrient flux, and suboxic conditions, contain significantly lower OM, highlighting paleoclimate as the primary control. Based on comprehensive NCB borehole data, organic carbon (OC) burial amounts were quantified, revealing a drastic decline from 4053.5 Gt C (1185.2 Gt C/Myr) in the Sakmarian to 1876.2 Gt C (275.9 Gt C/Myr) in the Artinskian. This represents a net reduction of 2177.3 Gt C in organic carbon burial from the Sakmarian to the Artinskian. We propose that this large-scale regional decline in carbon burial from the NCB acted as a significant positive feedback, exacerbating early Artinskian warming. The termination of the LPIA was therefore driven by a synergy of processes, where the collapse of the NCB carbon sink, coupled with global volcanic emissions and reduced weathering, collectively forced the climate system past a critical threshold, ending Earth’s longest-lived Phanerozoic icehouse. Published: 2026-07-17T23:17:29+00:00 Venue: Geoscience Frontiers Score: 0.568 (consider)

Journal: Geoscience Frontiers

Relevance: 0.568 (consider)

The mechanisms governing carbon cycle feedbacks during the demise of the Late Paleozoic Ice Age (LPIA) remain poorly constrained, particularly the role of regional carbon sinks transitioning to sources. The North China Block (NCB), once a major late Paleozoic carbon sink, experienced a dramatic decline in carbon sequestration during the Sakmarian–Artinskian, coincident with global deglaciation. However, the drivers of this regional shift and its potential contribution to the LPIA termination are poorly constrained. To address this, an integrated petrological, sedimentological, and geochemical study was conducted on mudstones from the Dacheng coalfield, Hebei Province, establishing an organic matter (OM) enrichment model for the coastal plain to alluvial plain transition. Results indicate that Sakmarian coastal plain mudstones, deposited under warm-humid conditions with enhanced nutrient supply and suboxic-anoxic waters, host higher OM. In contrast, Artinskian alluvial plain deposits, influenced by climatic aridification, reduced nutrient flux, and suboxic conditions, contain significantly lower OM, highlighting paleoclimate as the primary control. Based on comprehensive NCB borehole data, organic carbon (OC) burial amounts were quantified, revealing a drastic decline from 4053.5 Gt C (1185.2 Gt C/Myr) in the Sakmarian to 1876.2 Gt C (275.9 Gt C/Myr) in the Artinskian. This represents a net reduction of 2177.3 Gt C in organic carbon burial from the Sakmarian to the Artinskian. We propose that this large-scale regional decline in carbon burial from the NCB acted as a significant positive feedback, exacerbating early Artinskian warming. The termination of the LPIA was therefore driven by a synergy of processes, where the collapse of the NCB carbon sink, coupled with global volcanic emissions and reduced weathering, collectively forced the climate system past a critical threshold, ending Earth’s longest-lived Phanerozoic icehouse.

item.title: Petrogenesis of ∼1.96 Ga Mafic–Intermediate Intrusions in the Daqingshan Area: Constraints on the Tectonic Evolution of the Khondalite Belt, North China Craton
item.dc:creator: Yundi Gong
Xiaojie Dong
Zhongyuan Xu
Guanghui Lian
Zhenghong Liu
Guoyu Fan
item.description: The Khondalite Belt (KB) is a Paleoproterozoic collisional orogen within the North China Craton (NCC), but its pre-collisional tectonic evolution remains poorly constrained. In this study, we conducted integrated geochronological, in-situ Hf isotopic, and whole-rock geochemical analyses of the mafic–intermediate intrusions exposed in the Daqingshan area in the central segment of the KB to determine their formation ages, petrogenesis, and tectonic setting, thereby further constraining the late Paleoproterozoic tectonic evolution of the KB. Zircon U-Pb dating results show that these rocks crystallized at ∼1.97–1.96 Ga and subsequently underwent metamorphism at ∼1.94–1.92 Ga. These rocks are enriched in light rare earth elements and depleted in high field strength elements, exhibiting geochemical affinities comparable to those of continental margin arc basalts and island arc basalts. In-situ zircon Hf isotopic analyses yield positive εHf(t) values. The available data suggest that the mafic–intermediate intrusions were derived from a hybrid mantle source dominated by asthenospheric mantle and metasomatized by fluids derived from a subducted slab and experienced fractional crystallization of olivine, clinopyroxene, Fe-Ti oxides and apatite. Overall, these findings suggest that the KB was in a subduction-related tectonic setting at ∼1.97–1.96 Ga. Subsequent continent–continent collision at ∼1.95 Ga resulted in metamorphism of both the magmatic rocks and the Khondalite Series at ∼1.94–1.92 Ga. Published: 2026-07-16T15:44:11+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.566 (consider)

Relevance: 0.566 (consider)

The Khondalite Belt (KB) is a Paleoproterozoic collisional orogen within the North China Craton (NCC), but its pre-collisional tectonic evolution remains poorly constrained. In this study, we conducted integrated geochronological, in-situ Hf isotopic, and whole-rock geochemical analyses of the mafic–intermediate intrusions exposed in the Daqingshan area in the central segment of the KB to determine their formation ages, petrogenesis, and tectonic setting, thereby further constraining the late Paleoproterozoic tectonic evolution of the KB. Zircon U-Pb dating results show that these rocks crystallized at ∼1.97–1.96 Ga and subsequently underwent metamorphism at ∼1.94–1.92 Ga. These rocks are enriched in light rare earth elements and depleted in high field strength elements, exhibiting geochemical affinities comparable to those of continental margin arc basalts and island arc basalts. In-situ zircon Hf isotopic analyses yield positive εHf(t) values. The available data suggest that the mafic–intermediate intrusions were derived from a hybrid mantle source dominated by asthenospheric mantle and metasomatized by fluids derived from a subducted slab and experienced fractional crystallization of olivine, clinopyroxene, Fe-Ti oxides and apatite. Overall, these findings suggest that the KB was in a subduction-related tectonic setting at ∼1.97–1.96 Ga. Subsequent continent–continent collision at ∼1.95 Ga resulted in metamorphism of both the magmatic rocks and the Khondalite Series at ∼1.94–1.92 Ga.

item.title: Early Jurassic back-arc basin development in the South Qiangtang terrane, Tibetan Plateau: A sedimentary response to the northward subduction of the Meso-Tethys Ocean
item.dc:creator: Yanshen Liu
Zhongjie Xu
Chaoming Xie
Shiyao Gao
Dazhi Xu
item.description: The impact of the northward subduction of the Meso-Tethys Ocean (Bangong-Nujiang Tethyan Ocean) on the tectonic-sedimentary evolution of the South Qiangtang terrane remains poorly constrained. In particular, the lack of systematic studies on sedimentary records within this subduction system has hindered a deeper understanding of the oceanic basin evolution and the oceanic-continental subduction processes. Here, we present an integrated investigation of sedimentology, petrography, zircon Rare Earth Elements geochemistry, and detrital zircon U-Pb geochronology for the Sewa Formation in the Rialuma section along the southern margin of the South Qiangtang terrane and systematically reveal the depositional environment, provenance characteristics, and tectonic setting of the Sewa Formation. Results indicate that the maximum depositional age of the Lower-Middle Jurassic Sewa Formation is constrained to the Early Jurassic (∼177 Ma). Its lower member consists of barrier island-lagoon deposits, while its upper member is dominated by open-shelf deposits. By comparing detrital zircon U-Pb age spectra and performing multi-dimensional scaling analysis with data from Lower Jurassic clastic rocks along the southern margin of Laurasia, we identify the primary sediment sources. These include the South and North Qiangtang terranes, the Longmu Co-Shuanghu Suture Zone (an accretionary complex), and the Amdo microcontinent. Using the Eu anomaly in detrital zircons, we reconstruct the crustal thickness evolution of the South Qiangtang terrane from 260 to 170 Ma. Further, the r _Dz index indicates that the Early Jurassic Sewa Formation was deposited in a continent-continent collision tectonic setting. Synthesizing these new data with previous studies on the Sewa Formation, we propose that the study area was situated in a back-arc basin during the Early Jurassic, with collision and amalgamation occurring between the Amdo microcontinent and South Qiangtang terrane. These findings provide new sedimentary evidence for the Early Jurassic tectonic evolution of the eastern segment of the Meso-Tethys Ocean. Published: 2026-07-20T01:36:02+00:00 Venue: Science China Earth Sciences Score: 0.563 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yanshen Liu; Zhongjie Xu; Chaoming Xie; Shiyao Gao; Dazhi Xu

Journal: Science China Earth Sciences

DOI: 10.1007/s11430-025-2002-7

Relevance: 0.563 (consider)

The impact of the northward subduction of the Meso-Tethys Ocean (Bangong-Nujiang Tethyan Ocean) on the tectonic-sedimentary evolution of the South Qiangtang terrane remains poorly constrained. In particular, the lack of systematic studies on sedimentary records within this subduction system has hindered a deeper understanding of the oceanic basin evolution and the oceanic-continental subduction processes. Here, we present an integrated investigation of sedimentology, petrography, zircon Rare Earth Elements geochemistry, and detrital zircon U-Pb geochronology for the Sewa Formation in the Rialuma section along the southern margin of the South Qiangtang terrane and systematically reveal the depositional environment, provenance characteristics, and tectonic setting of the Sewa Formation. Results indicate that the maximum depositional age of the Lower-Middle Jurassic Sewa Formation is constrained to the Early Jurassic (∼177 Ma). Its lower member consists of barrier island-lagoon deposits, while its upper member is dominated by open-shelf deposits. By comparing detrital zircon U-Pb age spectra and performing multi-dimensional scaling analysis with data from Lower Jurassic clastic rocks along the southern margin of Laurasia, we identify the primary sediment sources. These include the South and North Qiangtang terranes, the Longmu Co-Shuanghu Suture Zone (an accretionary complex), and the Amdo microcontinent. Using the Eu anomaly in detrital zircons, we reconstruct the crustal thickness evolution of the South Qiangtang terrane from 260 to 170 Ma. Further, the r _Dz index indicates that the Early Jurassic Sewa Formation was deposited in a continent-continent collision tectonic setting. Synthesizing these new data with previous studies on the Sewa Formation, we propose that the study area was situated in a back-arc basin during the Early Jurassic, with collision and amalgamation occurring between the Amdo microcontinent and South Qiangtang terrane. These findings provide new sedimentary evidence for the Early Jurassic tectonic evolution of the eastern segment of the Meso-Tethys Ocean.

item.title: Metallogeny of the Mesoproterozoic Belt-Purcell Basin, Northern Rocky Mountains, USA-Canada
item.dc:creator: John F. Slack
item.description: Abstract Metasedimentary rocks of the Mesoproterozoic Belt Supergroup in the United States and correlative Purcell Supergroup in Canada contain diverse types of mineral deposits with a variety of commodities including base metals, precious metals, and critical metals. The economically most important deposits (≥ 10 Mt past production + reserves + resources) are stratabound Pb–Zn–Ag ores at the Sullivan mine in southeastern British Columbia, Ag–Pb–Zn–(Cu) veins of the Coeur d’Alene district in northern Idaho, stratabound sediment-hosted Cu–Ag deposits of the Spar Lake, Montanore, and Rock Creek deposits in western Montana, stratabound Co–Cu–Au–(Bi–Y–REE) ores of the Idaho cobalt belt, and stratiform Cu–Co–Ag at the Black Butte deposit in western Montana. Minor deposits in the region, also hosted in strata of the Belt and Purcell supergroups, are stratabound Pb–Zn–Ag deposits in southeastern British Columbia, and Th–REE–(Cu) veins of the Lemhi Pass district in northern Idaho and western Montana. Small prospects and occurrences include gold-quartz veins, Ag–Pb–Zn veins, Fe–(Cu–Au) deposits, and a variety of rare metals (Th, REE, Nb, Be, Sn) in pegmatites and carbonatites. High-precision geochronology of ore and gangue minerals, like in the Sullivan deposit, has yielded only Mesoproterozoic ages, but other deposits (Blackbird, Rock Creek, Coeur d’Alene, Lemhi Pass) have U–Pb and/or Re–Os ages that suggest Neoproterozoic, Paleozoic, and/or Mesozoic to Tertiary overprinting of older Mesoproterozoic mineralization, possibly linked in some cases to fluid flow during reactivation of deep basement structures. Some of the Paleozoic and younger ages are also attributed here to dissolution-reprecipitation of dated minerals and not to new metal introduction. An integrated assessment of the geochronological data reveals growing evidence for previously underappreciated roles of major mineralizing events occurring during the East Kootenay orogeny (1379 − 1325 Ma) and the Grenvillian orogeny (1150 to 980 Ma), with important implications for mineral exploration in many parts of the Belt-Purcell Basin. Published: 2026-07-17T07:55:50+00:00 Venue: Mineralium Deposita Score: 0.562 (consider)

Abstract Metasedimentary rocks of the Mesoproterozoic Belt Supergroup in the United States and correlative Purcell Supergroup in Canada contain diverse types of mineral deposits with a variety of commodities including base metals, precious metals, and critical metals. The economically most important deposits (≥ 10 Mt past production + reserves + resources) are stratabound Pb–Zn–Ag ores at the Sullivan mine in southeastern British Columbia, Ag–Pb–Zn–(Cu) veins of the Coeur d’Alene district in northern Idaho, stratabound sediment-hosted Cu–Ag deposits of the Spar Lake, Montanore, and Rock Creek deposits in western Montana, stratabound Co–Cu–Au–(Bi–Y–REE) ores of the Idaho cobalt belt, and stratiform Cu–Co–Ag at the Black Butte deposit in western Montana. Minor deposits in the region, also hosted in strata of the Belt and Purcell supergroups, are stratabound Pb–Zn–Ag deposits in southeastern British Columbia, and Th–REE–(Cu) veins of the Lemhi Pass district in northern Idaho and western Montana. Small prospects and occurrences include gold-quartz veins, Ag–Pb–Zn veins, Fe–(Cu–Au) deposits, and a variety of rare metals (Th, REE, Nb, Be, Sn) in pegmatites and carbonatites.
